Using data from the 1982 and 2014 PNAD and the 2016, 2019, and 2024 PNAD Contínua, we adopt a positional approach that measures parents’ and children’s education in terms of cohort-specific percentile ranks rather than absolute years of schooling. To address the discrete nature of educational attainment, we estimate kernel-smoothed joint distributions of parental and offspring education and calculate upward and downward transition probabilities by race. This strategy allows us to compare racial inequalities in educational mobility over a long historical period marked by profound educational expansion, including the diffusion of basic schooling, the massification of secondary and higher education, and recent affirmative action policies. The results show that Whites consistently enjoyed greater chances of upward mobility and lower risks of downward mobility than non-Whites. Racial gaps in upward mobility narrowed across intermediate and recent cohorts, especially among those originating at the bottom of the distribution, but remained far from eliminated. By contrast, racial inequalities in downward mobility proved more persistent and robust over time. Analyses by sex indicate that the recent convergence in downward mobility is explained mainly by the advances made by non-White women. Taken together, educational expansion reduced, but did not overcome, racial inequality in intergenerational mobility in Brazil.

title: Addressing Rural Unemployment Disparities in Oregon: A Spatial Analysis and Multi-Criteria Policy Evaluation
abstract: Oregon's unemployment rate of 5.2% in February 2026 exceeds the national average of 4.4% and has diverged from national trends since June 2024. County-level Bureau of Labor Statistics data reveals a 4.5 percentage point spread across Oregon's 36 counties, with the highest rates concentrated in rural eastern and southern counties. This analysis uses ArcGIS Online spatial visualization and a Weighted Sum Model Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis to evaluate three workforce policy alternatives. Remote-friendly small business incentives received the highest weighted score (4.05), supported by SBA Office of Advocacy data showing small businesses employ 54.3% of rural workers nationally. This analysis recommends Oregon establish a Rural Remote-Friendly Small Business Incentive Program targeting residents of high-unemployment rural counties starting or expanding businesses that can operate online or serve clients remotely.

title: Designing for the Threshold: Relational Outcomes and Inclusive Scaffolding in Higher Education Inductions — The FRIEND Feasibility Study
abstract: UK student loneliness has become a persistent concern in higher education, especially around induction and early transition points. Higher education institutions increasingly use brief relational primers to support connection, yet such activities are rarely compared head-to-head in pre-registered feasibility trials and are even less often evaluated against explicit inclusive-design safeguards for anxious or neurodivergent students. FRIEND is a pre-registered, CONSORT-aligned cluster-feasibility randomised controlled trial comparing a 15-minute Nonviolent Communication primer with a duration-matched mindfulness body-scan control in 30 students organised into 15 stranger dyads. All four a priori progression criteria were met: retention 100%, protocol adherence 100%, adverse events 0, and a directional primary-outcome signal on dyadic closeness. Across the three convergent analytic pathways, the NVC primer showed a consistent directional advantage over the mindfulness control on IOS change scores. The Mann–Whitney test yielded U = 160.5, p = .041, rrb = .43, indicating a medium effect in favour of NVC. The random-intercept mixed model estimated b = 1.06, 95% CI [0.13, 1.99], p = .025, after accounting for dyadic clustering. A BCa bootstrap with R = 10,000 (seed 20260425) returned rrb = .43 with a 95% CI of [−0.74, +0.005]. Taken together, these estimates are consistent with a directional mechanism signal of feasibility-grade magnitude rather than definitive evidence of effectiveness. Descriptively, the scaffold-usage pattern was compatible with a calm–connection divergence, in which procedural calm and relational closeness did not map perfectly onto the same condition. The study provides a transparent template for testing relational primers and perceived-safety scaffolding in induction, peer-support, and buddy-programme settings.

title: Social Culture in the Era of Generative AI: Structural Transformation, Cognitive Reshaping, and Future Perspectives
abstract: The rapid development of generative artificial intelligence (GAI) is profoundly reshaping the fundamental structure of human society and culture. From knowledge production methods to daily communication patterns, from cultural content creation to value formation mechanisms, AI is transforming from a "tool-based technology" into a "cultural force." This paper systematically analyzes the impact mechanism of generative AI on contemporary social culture from four dimensions: social structure, cultural production, identity recognition, and ethical order, and further explores the cognitive reshaping and future development trends it has triggered. The study argues that generative AI not only changes the efficiency of cultural dissemination but also restructures the relationship between "humans, technology, and culture," enabling social culture to enter a new stage of "co-generation."

title: Autonomy, Adolescents, and Artificial Intelligence
abstract: In this essay, forthcoming 32 B.U. J. Sci. & Tech. L. (2026), I explore the potential implications of adolescent use of AI assistants on the development of adolescents’ capacities for autonomy. Thinking through these issues from the perspective of first principles is, I believe, needed in order to sharpen our understanding of some of the issues AI assistants pose for young people. A clear analysis will suggest which legal or policy prescriptions should follow. I first give an overview of adolescent use of AI assistants, noting their popularity and the variety of use contexts. Drawing upon the work of Catriona Mackenzie, I then outline a relational conception of autonomy that conceives of autonomy as having three distinct dimensions: self-determination, self-governance, and self-authorization. The precision of Mackenzie’s conception allows us to more carefully consider how different uses and contexts of technological use can affect individuals’ capacities for autonomy. Using this three-dimensional conception of autonomy, I propose three potential interventions for law and policy: thinking beyond harms, substantive privacy protections, and autonomy impact assessments.

title: Inside of Structural Economy: An Approach from Informal Angola Economy.
abstract: This approach focuses specifically on the behaviour of the economy, that is, its economic structure, with particular emphasis on the formal and informal sectors. Thus, the evidence points specifically to the plausible existence of an informal economy in Angola, the significance of which is in fact closely linked to the very nature of the determining factors that are, by their very nature, relevant. A key feature of the informal economic structure relates, above all, to the fact that there is, for example, a limited capacity on the part of the state to generate wealth, as evidenced by significant tax losses; for instance, Angola incurs an annual tax loss of 6.9 billion dollars. On the other hand, however, it nevertheless represents a significant opportunity cost in terms of public policy; thus, the analysis suggests that, to the extent that there is a substantial expansion of the informal economy, on the other hand, there is a particular trend towards the continuous emergence of new informal professions. Informal markets exhibit characteristics that are specific to them; for example, most informal workers do not have fixed monthly incomes, and income levels depend directly on daily informal economic activities. Thus, the greater the flow of informal economic activities, the higher the income levels of economic agents will naturally be. On the other hand, approach show in particular, that, many Underdevelopment Countries, they not give attention to informal economy, where, on majority of this countries see the informal economy as statistics, the politics and institutions not exist in these Countries, in particular.

title: The Ethnic Reputations of Neighborhoods: A Study of Mainstream and Social Media Discourse in Sweden
authors: Miriam Hurtado Bodell, Benjamin Jarvis, Selcan Mutgan
abstract: This study introduces ethnic reputations—collectively constructed associations between specific neighborhoods and ethnic groups—as a distinct dimension of urban symbolic reality. These reputations circulate in public discourse and may guide the race- and ethnicity-based heuristics people use to evaluate places, serving as potential mediators in segregation processes. A key question is whether ethnic reputations correspond to the demographic realities of neighborhoods. We answer this question by applying computational methods to a combination of textual and demographic data. We use word embeddings models to extract ethnic reputation scores for ethnic groups and neighborhoods in Stockholm from articles in Sweden's largest newspaper and posts in its largest online discussion forum between 2010 and 2017. We link these to demographic data from the Swedish population registers, enabling comparisons of ethnic reputations to demographic reality across media environments. We find that ethnic reputations appear in both media environments and follow similar patterns: reputations tend to be stronger for groups perceived as more culturally distant from the Swedish majority, especially in neighborhoods with larger minority populations. However, reputations in social media are more consistent across neighborhoods, groups, and over time compared to mainstream media. These results show that media discourses contain ethnicized representations of urban space that are accessible to a wider public and only partially reflect demographic realities.

title: Environmental Citizen Science in India: Objectives, Challenges, and Impacts
abstract: Environmental Citizen Science (ECS) - involving community members in the research addressing environmental and conservation challenges - is widely promoted for targeted data collection and analysis at broader spatial and temporal scales, and to encourage public engagement with science. Keeping perspectives and experiences of the coordinators of 22 ECS from India in focus this study explored the purpose of the projects, challenges faced during implementation, and their contribution to the science and society. Most of the ECS we studied were contributory in nature and had dual targets: biodiversity data generation and strengthening societal involvement in science. The role of coordinators in ECS in India was a mix of boundary worker and knowledge broker. Minimal availability of resources, reduced participation from the rural regions, and inconsistency in involvement and contribution of the citizen scientists were the major challenges ECS faced. Large-scale collection of biodiversity data, and increased visibility of the taxa and ecosystems were the important outcomes of ECS reported by the coordinators. However, assessment of learning by the participants and evaluation of the social impact of the ECS were not common. The need for developing and implementing an all-inclusive support mechanism and evidence-based policy framework to ensure the efficacy and sustainability of the ECS in India is discussed in the light of the present findings.

title: Unequal Legacies: A Comparative Spatial Analysis of UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage and World Heritage Site Inscriptions
abstract: UNESCO's World Heritage Sites (WHS) and Intangible Cultural Heritage (ICH) lists represent the most ambitious international effort to identify and safeguard humanity's cultural legacy. Yet the geographic distribution of inscriptions across these two programmes and the degree to which that distribution aligns with UNESCO's stated representational equity goals remains insufficiently examined. This study provides a systematic comparative spatial analysis of all ICH and WHS inscriptions, employing fractional weighting to address the methodological challenge of transnational nominations. Results show a significant and structured heritage divide. WHS inscriptions are nearly twice as unequally distributed as ICH, with Europe and North America accounting for 46.5% of all weighted WHS while holding only 28.4% of ICH. Africa faces compounding disadvantages across both lists, with 59.5% of its 42 member states classified as double-underrepresented. Arab States present a paradox of high per-state heritage efficiency alongside the highest WHS danger proportion (21.9%), concentrated in conflict-affected states. These findings shed light to the fact that UNESCO's representational equity goals are unevenly met across both programmes, and that the structural asymmetries between the two lists are as analytically important as their individual distributions.

title: РЕГИОНАЛЬНЫЕ ОНЛАЙН-ПЛОЩАДКИ ОБЪЯВЛЕНИЙ КАК ДРАЙВЕР ПРЕДПРИНИМАТЕЛЬСТВА: ВЛИЯНИЕ НА ТРАНЗАКЦИОННЫЕ ИЗДЕРЖКИ, ЗАНЯТОСТЬ И ФОРМАЛИЗАЦИЮ БИЗНЕСА (Regional Online Classifieds Platforms as a Driver of Entrepreneurship: Impact on Transaction Costs, Employment, and Business Formalization)
abstract: Abstract. The purpose of this study is to comprehensively analyze the impact of regional online classifieds platforms on entrepreneurship development in Russian regions. This research is conducted through the lens of reducing transaction costs, stimulating employment, and formalizing business. The main findings of the study show that active use of online classifieds platforms reduces transaction costs by 23-37%, contributes to the creation of additional jobs, and encourages entrepreneurs to transition from the informal sector to the legal one. The findings confirm the significant role of digital platforms in transforming the regional entrepreneurial ecosystem and shaping new business models. Keywords: online classifieds, transaction costs, regional entrepreneurship, digitalization of the economy, business formalization, employment, e-commerce, small business.

title: ИСКУССТВЕННЫЙ ИНТЕЛЛЕКТ КАК ИНСТРУМЕНТ ПОВЫШЕНИЯ ЭФФЕКТИВНОСТИ ПРОИЗВОДСТВА: ОПЫТ РЕАЛЬНОГО БИЗНЕСА (Artificial Intelligence as a Tool for Enhancing Production Efficiency: Real-World Business Experience)
abstract: Abstract. In the current context of digital transformation, solutions based on artificial intelligence (AI) help enhance the organization of production processes, minimize waste and defects, reduce equipment downtime, improve product quality control, and yield a range of other benefits associated with increased production efficiency. This article examines the current potential for integrating AI into core and auxiliary production processes. The possibilities for applying AI to the manufacturing of automotive wheels are explored. Based on an analysis of practical experience, conclusions are drawn regarding the challenges and future prospects for implementing AI in manufacturing. This work holds practical significance for specialists in the fields of production management and strategic planning. Keywords: economic development, production process optimization, artificial intelligence, production efficiency, real-world business experience.

title: Стратегии глобального масштабирования бизнеса в производстве автомобильных дисков: от локального успеха к международной экспансии (Global Business Scaling Strategies in Automotive Wheel Manufacturing: From Local Success to International Expansion)
abstract: Abstract. The primary objective of any business is to generate the necessary profit to ensure sustainable long-term development. In the context of globalization and intensifying competition, a strategy of international scaling serves as one effective means of achieving this goal. This article examines methodological approaches to global business expansion, using the automotive wheel manufacturing industry as a case study. It outlines an algorithm for a scaling strategy, characterizes the most significant factors influencing the effectiveness of entering foreign markets, and analyzes various growth models (export-based models, the formation of strategic partnerships, and mergers and acquisitions). Finally, the article formulates recommendations for international development specifically tailored to companies operating in the automotive wheel manufacturing sector. Keywords: economic development, business development strategies, business scaling, automotive wheel manufacturing, international business expansion.

title: Управление рисками в реальном секторе с использованием цифровых решений: прогнозирование, аналитика и контроль (Risk Management in the Real Sector Using Digital Solutions: Forecasting, Analytics, and Control)
abstract: Abstract: As the foundation of the economic system, the real sector is particularly susceptible to risks associated with shifting market conditions, technological failures, fluctuations in resource prices, and regulatory and geopolitical factors. In the current environment, effective risk management has assumed critical importance and necessitates the utilization of advanced digital technologies. This article explores the potential for applying digital solutions to risk management within the real sector. It outlines general approaches to risk management in the real sector and describes the digital transformation tools applicable to this field. The methodological potential for risk forecasting, analysis, and control—facilitated by digital technologies—is characterized. Furthermore, key directions for the digitalization of risk management are identified. The findings of this study can be utilized in formulating programs aimed at enhancing the resilience of real-sector enterprises and minimizing the adverse consequences of economic uncertainty. Keywords: economic development, risk management, real sector, digital solutions, forecasting, analytics, control.

title: УПРАВЛЕНИЕ ЦЕПОЧКАМИ ПОСТАВОК В МЕЖДУНАРОДНОМ БИЗНЕСЕ: ВЫЗОВЫ И РЕШЕНИЯ В ПРОИЗВОДСТВЕ И ЭКСПОРТЕ АВТОМОБИЛЬНЫХ ДИСКОВ (Supply Chain Management in International Business: Challenges and Solutions in the Production and Export of Automotive Wheels)
abstract: The sphere of production and export of car wheels is characterized by trends of globalization, development of digital technologies, changes in logistics routes and increasing requirements for sustainable development. In this context, the use of economically sound tools in organizing supply chain management can provide a company with significant competitive advantages. The article considers current problems and ways to eliminate them in relation to manufacturers and exporters of car wheels. The methodological basis for supply chain management in international business is considered, the most significant risks in the management of supply chains of car wheels are described, solutions for optimizing these processes are given (diversification of suppliers and logistics, implementation of digital technologies, automation of logistics processes, development of strategic partnerships with suppliers, adaptation to the requirements of sustainable development). The results of the study have the potential for methodological and applied application in the field of international business and logistics. Key words: economic development, supply chain management, international business, production of automobile wheels, wheel export.

title: Лидерство в производственном бизнесе: как предприниматели внедряют цифровую культуру в традиционные отрасли (Leadership in Manufacturing: How Entrepreneurs Implement a Digital Culture in Traditional Industries)
abstract: Abstract: The specific nature of modern business organization suggests that digitalization is no longer merely a trend, but a vital necessity that determines a company's success in the marketplace. It impacts not only high-tech sectors but is also becoming an integral factor in the development of traditional industrial spheres. In this context, the fundamental driver of successful transformation is leadership—specifically, the ability of entrepreneurs to effectively manage the implementation of digital culture initiatives as part of their strategic business development. This article explores the issues surrounding the integration of digital culture into the operations of manufacturing companies. The author examines methodological approaches to digital transformation, analyzes the most significant factors influencing this process, identifies common barriers and risks, and discusses strategic solutions for integrating digital culture. The paper addresses challenges related to the evolution of management styles and methods, the cultivation of a digital mindset, and the transformation of corporate culture. Finally, it outlines the prospects for digital leadership within traditional industrial sectors. Keywords: economic development, business leadership, manufacturing business, digital culture, corporate culture, industry leadership prospects.

title: МИРОВОЙ ОПЫТ ОПТИМИЗАЦИИ ТРАНСПОРТНЫХ СЕТЕЙ ГОРОДСКИХ АГЛОМЕРАЦИЙ (Global Experience in Optimizing Transport Networks in Urban Agglomerations)
abstract: This article outlines the primary methods for addressing transport-related challenges within urban agglomerations. It identifies potential solutions to these problems, drawing upon global experience in reducing traffic intensity within urban areas. Particular emphasis is placed on prioritizing measures aimed at organizing and developing high-performance off-street passenger transport systems, particularly in suburban districts. Keywords: urban agglomeration, transport network development, urban agglomeration transport infrastructure, measures to reduce traffic flow intensity, global experience in addressing transport network congestion.

title: Экономические и организационные предпосылки развития транспортной сети (Economic and Organizational Prerequisites for the Development of the Transport Network)
abstract: This article outlines the distinctive features and identifies the key challenges facing the transport network of the Moscow Urban Agglomeration. The underlying causes of transport-related problems are determined based on a comprehensive approach to the development of transport infrastructure across Moscow and the Moscow Region. Priority directions for the development of the Moscow agglomeration’s transport network are highlighted, and a system of indicators is presented to facilitate the evaluation of various network development scenarios from the perspectives of both social and commercial efficiency. Keywords: urban agglomeration, transport network, transport network development, transport network development efficiency

title: Проблемы транспортных сетей мегаполисов (Problems of transport networks of megacities)
abstract: The article notes the features of the development of megacities and their transport component, as an integral element of the functioning of large cities. Emphasis is placed on the priority development of public passenger transport. The priority of measures to improve the organization of traffic is emphasized. Possible ways to solve transport problems have been identified

title: Die historisch-soziologische Erforschung der Abel-Biogramme: Methodologische Notizen, Anmerkungen zur Verwendung von AI in der qualitativen Sozialforschung und ein Ergebnis der Fallanalysen
abstract: Dieser Beitrag versammelt Forschungsnotizen, die sich durch die Analysen der Nazi-Biogramme, die im Kontext des Preissauschreibens von T. F. Abel 1934 entstanden sind, ergeben haben. Das Ziel ist es, zukünftigen Forschungen die Arbeit zu erleichtern und einige neue Informationen zum Gegenstand beizusteuern. Der erste Teil behandelt Herausforderungen im Umgang mit dem Datenkorpus und Ansätze dafür, AI-Tools für die qualitative Forschung zu nutzen. Im zweiten Teil werden forschungsstrategische und methodologische Fragen besprochen. Im dritten Teil wird als inhaltliches Ergebnis der Fallanalysen erläutert, dass eine eigentümliche Dialektik von Agency und Fatalismus in der nationalsozialistischen Weltanschauung eine zentrale Rolle spielen.

title: Scrolling Through the Plate: Motivations Behind Why Young Adults Post Food Pictures on Social Media - A Qualitative Study from New Zealand
abstract: Background: Food photography has become a defining behaviour of young adults on social media, yet the motivations underlying this practice remain incompletely understood. Existing literature has examined photo-sharing broadly, but few studies have explored the specific intentions behind food picture posting through qualitative inquiry in the Oceania context. Objective: To investigate the intentions and psychosocial motivations behind why young adults post food-related pictures on social media platforms. Methods: A qualitative approach using semi-structured interviews was employed. Fifteen young adults aged 18 to 25 years, residing in Auckland, New Zealand, participated. Purposive and opportunistic sampling strategies were used. Participants shared five food pictures they had personally posted on social media, and interviews explored their motivations. Thematic analysis following the Braun and Clarke (2006) framework was applied to the data. Results: Four principal themes emerged to explain why young adults post food pictures: (1) Cooked food - sharing home-prepared food to express pride and seek social validation; (2) Pretty food - sharing aesthetically appealing food driven by visual appeal and associated social reasons; (3) The highlight of the day - capturing food as a memory of significant moments, meetings, cultural occasions, or as miscellaneous social expression; and (4) Exotic food - sharing food that is rare or unavailable in one's country of origin. Additional behavioural dimensions included the immediacy of posting and platform preference, with Instagram favoured over Facebook among this cohort. Conclusion: Young adults post food pictures on social media for a range of psychosocial reasons extending beyond aesthetics. Attribution theory, social gratification, and collective or we-intention frameworks offer explanatory lenses. These findings contribute to the growing body of knowledge on food-related social media behaviour among young adults and have implications for health communication and digital food literacy.

title: Divergence of Economic Opportunity in Average Places: Pathways of Stagnation
authors: Howard Hao Wang, Harald Bathelt, Maximilian Buchholz
abstract: Widening interregional inequality and growing political divides have led to a growing body of work investigating extreme cases of economic growth and decline. In contrast, our research of the U.S. urban system analyzes ‘average places’, defined as urban regions with less than 250,000 people and average income levels in 1993. This analysis focuses on places with low growth between 1993 and 2017 and complements an earlier paper that investigates high-growth average places. Using fuzzy-set qualitative comparative analysis (fsQCA), we identify three trajectories of stagnation with different absent or present socio-economic and institutional conditions. These are (i) isolated manufacturing/nonspecialized towns, (ii) satellite manufacturing/non-specialized towns, and (iii) disconnecting publicsector/non-specialized towns. Our results suggest that low-growth average places are more concentrated in a limited number of trajectories than their high-growth counterparts, and that they are largely characterized by the absence of conditions, as opposed to their presence. This is not simply a consequence of deindustrialization but must be understood in combination with other context conditions.

title: Mapping Australia’s Regulatory Architecture: A Seven-Layer Census of Regulatory Authority
abstract: Australia’s regulatory system is large, fragmented, and structurally opaque. No jurisdiction maintains a complete inventory of its regulators, and no national body tracks the full extent of regulatory authority across the federation. This preprint presents a cross‑jurisdictional census of regulatory authority in Australia using a seven‑layer architecture model. The method identifies and classifies regulators across statutory agencies, embedded units, national schemes, tribunals, and forms of regulatory dark matter. The findings indicate that Australia operates between 1,244 and 1,404 regulatory bodies and loci of authority, with a central estimate of approximately 1,360. Every jurisdiction, regardless of population size, operates a triple‑digit regulatory system. The study contributes a replicable census method, a seven‑layer model of regulatory architecture, and an account of how regulatory authority is distributed across jurisdictions and institutional layers. These findings have important implications for regulatory governance, visibility, coordination, and system stewardship.

title: Comparing Campaign Rhetoric and Legislative Behavior Through Issue-Specific Polarization in the US House of Representatives, 2021–2025
abstract: Voters make decisions on who to elect based, in part, upon candidate messaging and party platforms. Here we examine the relationship between campaign rhetoric and legislative behavior among members of the 117th and 118th United States House of Representatives. Using campaign text and roll-call voting data, the paper compares polarization across policy domains through an IssueIRT model of legislative behavior and a Wordscores model of campaign rhetoric. We find that polarization varies substantially across issue areas and that the relationship between rhetoric and behavior is not uniform. Civil Rights exhibits highly polarized and morally distinct partisan rhetoric, while Government Operations rhetoric displays a more continuous ideological spectrum despite strongly polarized voting behavior. International Affairs appears comparatively less polarized in both rhetoric and legislative behavior, with greater thematic overlap between parties surrounding national security and international engagement. We also observe a second latent dimension in congressional voting associated with institutionalism and intra-party factionalism. Republican legislators exhibit substantially greater dispersion along this dimension, while Democrats remain comparatively cohesive in both rhetoric and voting behavior.

title: Network analysis in advertising research, reimagined: A methodological catalyst for structural and institutional insight
authors: Alvin Zhou, harsh taneja, Ewa Maslowska
abstract: As digital systems increasingly mediate advertising, the field must move beyond message effects to engage with the structural and institutional dynamics that define the advertising ecosystem. This article argues that network analysis, when reimagined beyond its typical application to social media interactions, offers powerful tools for understanding the relationships among brands, consumers, media, influencers, and other intermediaries and stakeholders. Through two case demonstrations, one on influencer marketing and the other on media planning, we illustrate how bipartite projections and inferential network analysis uncover latent relational patterns such as brand competition and influencer heterophily. We further explore alternative strategies for constructing networks from advertising data, including sequence-based, co-occurrence-based, and influence-based approaches. These models enable a shift from descriptive to explanatory analyses and open new pathways for theorizing advertising as a relational, institutional, and dynamic system. Ultimately, we position network analysis as a methodological and theoretical catalyst for structural thinking in advertising research, facilitating deeper engagement with ecosystem-level complexity and interdisciplinary integration with computational social science.

title: Distinguishing Sustainability from Durability: Hidden Convergences and the Logic of Redirection
abstract: Sustainability and durability are often treated as overlapping terms in environmental debates. This article argues that distinguishing them reveals hidden convergences between positions usually presented as divergent. Sustainability evaluates trajectories under ecological limits, material dependence, limited substitutability, and systemic risk. Durability refers to the organized capacity of material and institutional arrangements to maintain a function through time, through maintenance, repair, support chains, and the governance of obsolescence. The argument is conceptually driven and empirically informed by an eight-month advisory study conducted by the author for a public development finance institution, involving twenty interviews, a 100-page internal report, and a 25-page synthesis. Drawing on Halloy’s contrast between living and zombie technologies, the article develops a sustainability–durability matrix distinguishing sustainable continuity, durable lock-in, sustainable fragility, and negative inheritances. It then uses redirection, organized withdrawal, and Deron’s framework of ruins, ghosts, seeds, and visions to rethink how inheritances may be maintained, supported, redirected, or allowed to decline.

title: Sensing the Research Field: A Somatic Methodology for Awareness-Based Collaborative Inquiry
authors: Louise Massacrier, Zoe Lina Wehmeyer, Marc Santolini
abstract: The complexity of contemporary challenges demands collaborative, interdisciplinary forms of knowledge production capable of integrating multiple ways of knowing. Yet the dominant research model brackets the conditions that make genuine collective inquiry possible — the quality of presence, the relational ground, the somatic attunement that builds trust and collective intelligence. This paper presents a methodological prototype that both implements and measures these conditions, drawing on two Social Presencing Theater practices: the Stuck practice as a daily somatic check-in building inter-researcher attunement, and 3D mapping as a collaborative material practice for project development and field sensing. Two researchers integrated both practices into an ongoing project on aesthetic sustainability and embodied learning in design. Results show statistically traceable change: posture shifts significantly toward openness within each session (p = 0.012), entry posture orientation shifts progressively inward across the research arc (p = 0.02–0.03), trust-coded language increases significantly after each practice (p = 0.023), and the affective valence of entry postures shifts from negative to positive over time. A compensatory within-session dynamic is also identified: the practice orients toward the affective quality latent in the entry posture rather than amplifying what is already present. Situated within the science of the social field and awareness-based design, we propose this prototype as a contribution to a knowledge production that values the quality of the process — the relational ground, the somatic attunement, the container built between researchers — as much as the outcomes it generates.

title: The Uncounted: A Case for Federal Enumeration of the Bereaved Parent Demographic in the United States
abstract: This research presents the legislative precedents and statutory arguments required to authorize the formal enumeration of the bereaved parent population within active federal surveys. It outlines the specific administrative instruments across federal agencies capable of restoring demographic tracking systems discontinued in 1885. This document is cross-indexed with the Social Science Research Network (SSRN) under registry metadata.

title: Формальное ядро цифрового поля Казахстана: контур, концентрация и пределы публичной видимости
abstract: The article reconstructs the formal core of Kazakhstan’s IT field through a series of empirical studies within the Digital Class research programme. Instead of treating the field as a sector composed of “IT companies plus IT workers”, it is analysed as the overlap of three maps: a formal OKED-based contour, an upper layer concentrated by taxpayer-level payment weight, and the regimes through which technical competence becomes publicly and professionally legible. The main findings are as follows. The formal contour includes approximately 32.5 thousand active entities selected by a fixed set of IT-related OKED codes. Almost half of the contour consists of individual entrepreneurs, while software development as a single OKED code accounts for about 40 percent of the contour. Payment weight is highly concentrated: a narrow upper layer - less than 1 percent of the contour - accounts for roughly two thirds of the aggregate payment trace, whereas about 44 percent of entities show no significant trace in the latest annual slice. Substantive classification of the upper layer shows that software production is only part of it, alongside enterprise implementation, embedded IT in non-software organizations, state digital infrastructure, and educational or symbolic layers. The article therefore argues that economic weight and public visibility are substantially decoupled. The data support claims about formal structure, payment-weight concentration and public readability; they do not support claims about revenue, profit, turnover, workforce size, influence, control or actual social mobility outcomes.

title: The Formal Core of Kazakhstan's Digital Field: Contour, Concentration, and the Limits of Public Visibility
abstract: This article reconstructs the formal core of Kazakhstan's IT field from a cumulative series of empirical pilots conducted within the Digital Class research programme. Instead of treating the field as an ordinary sector made up of IT firms plus IT workers, the article reconstructs it through three partially independent maps: the formal contour defined by official economic activity codes (OKED), the upper layer identified by taxpayer-level aggregate payment trace, and the regimes through which technical production becomes publicly and professionally legible. The analysis uses taxpayer-level aggregate payment traces as a proxy for relative payment weight. These traces are used only as shares and concentration indicators; no absolute payment amounts or entity-level payment tables are published. The article's main findings are as follows. First, the formal contour contains approximately 32,500 active entities selected through a fixed set of IT-related OKED codes. By count, the contour is more than half individual entrepreneurs, while software development as a single code accounts for roughly 40 percent of the contour. Second, payment weight is highly concentrated: a narrow upper layer of fewer than one percent of entities accounts for roughly two thirds of the aggregate payment trace, while about 44 percent of entities show no significant annual trace in the latest yearly slice. Third, manual classification of the upper layer shows that software production is only one component of the formal upper contour, alongside enterprise implementation, embedded IT functions in non-software organizations, state digital infrastructure and educational-symbolic actors. Finally, economic weight and public visibility are substantially decoupled. The article therefore treats the digital class not as a directly measurable population, but as a field object that becomes observable only through disciplined layering of formal, payment, production-boundary and visibility evidence. The data support claims about formal structure, payment concentration and public legibility. They do not support claims about revenue, profit, turnover, employment, influence, control, hidden networks or actual social mobility.

title: Das „politische Vorfeld“ als sekundäre Agitator:innen. Wie kann politische Online-Mobilisierung anhand einer zeitgemäßen Erweiterung des autoritären Sozialcharakters erklärt werden?
abstract: Diese Bachelorarbeit untersucht, wie politische Online-Mobilisierung durch Influencer:innen des sogenannten „politischen Vorfelds“ mithilfe einer zeitgemäßen Erweiterung der Sozialcharaktertheorie der Frankfurter Schule erklärt werden kann. Im Zentrum steht die Anfälligkeit von Individuen für faschistische Agitation im Zusammenspiel von Sozialcharakterstrukturen und agitatorischen Kommunikationsstrategien. Sozialcharakterstrukturen werden dabei als psychische Dispositionen bzw. Bedürfnisse gefasst, die durch sozioökonomische Bedingungen geprägt sind. Theoretisch rekonstruiert die Arbeit die Transformation vom autoritären hin zu einem narzisstisch-libertären Sozialcharakter, das gesteigerte Kränkungspotenzial spätmoderner Individuen sowie entsprechende Kompensationsmechanismen und daran anknüpfende Agitationsmethoden. Empirisch folgt die Studie einem qualitativen Forschungsdesign, das Netnography und strukturierende qualitative Inhaltsanalyse kombiniert. Am Fallbeispiel des Gesundheits-Influencers Fabian Kowallik wurden über einen Zeitraum von zwei Wochen Inhalte verschiedener Social-Media-Profile erhoben, dokumentiert und zusammen mit Nutzer:innen-Kommentaren analysiert. Letztere wurden zusätzlich mithilfe eines deduktiv-induktiv entwickelten Kategoriensystems auf Basis der Sozialcharaktertheorie mit MAXQDA ausgewertet. Die Analyse zeigt ein komplexes Zusammenspiel autoritärer und narzisstisch-libertärer Charakterstrukturen, wobei Mobilisierung im digitalen Raum insbesondere über affektive Bindung, Identifikation mit der Community sowie die Kompensation individueller Kränkungserfahrungen erfolgt. Influencer:innen fungieren dabei als sekundäre Agitator:innen, indem sie parasozial an narzisstische Kränkungspotenziale anschließen und durch emotionalisierende Agitationsmethoden – etwa Opfer-Märtyrer-Narrative, inszenierte Skandalisierungen sowie misstrauensbasierte und verschwörungsideologische Deutungsmuster – kollektiv-narzisstische Dynamiken fördern. Der innerhalb der jeweiligen Community ausgeprägte kollektive Narzissmus erweist sich im Kontext strategischer Netzwerkarbeit und algorithmischer Sichtbarkeitslogiken als anschlussfähig für neue Formen politischer Ein-flussnahme. Die Arbeit leistet damit einen Beitrag zur Weiterentwicklung der Autoritarismusforschung im Kontext sozialer Medien.

title: The Monopoly and Monetization of Academic Publication Rights: Toward Fairness in Scholarly Publishing Rights
abstract: With the highly commercialized development of the global academic publishing system, the right to publish papers has gradually evolved from a "knowledge dissemination mechanism" into a scarce resource controlled by a few publishing groups and platforms. In the current system, authors not only face high rejection rates and lengthy review processes during the submission stage, but also bear the dual pressure of high subscription fees and open access (OA) publication fees during the reading and dissemination stages. This structural asymmetry has led to the de facto "quasi-monopolization" and "monetization" of the right to publish papers. This paper analyzes the formation mechanism and economic logic of the monopoly of the right to publish papers, starting from the institutional structure of academic publishing, and further explores the practical dilemmas faced by preprint platforms, the open access movement, and new academic dissemination technologies. Finally, this paper proposes a reform path towards fairness, including decentralized review mechanisms, the reconstruction of the publicly funded publishing system and the academic evaluation system, in order to promote the evolution of the academic dissemination system towards a more open and equitable direction.

title: Is Openness in Preprint Platforms Truly Open? A Critical Reflection on the Fairness of Academic Publication Rights
abstract: Preprint platforms are widely regarded as an important mechanism for breaking down traditional academic publishing barriers and promoting the rapid dissemination of research results. However, beneath the surface of "open access," preprint systems still suffer from implicit screening mechanisms, differences in platform rules, and structural barriers caused by inequalities in resources and reputation. This paper analyzes the true boundaries of the "openness" of preprint platforms from the perspectives of their access mechanisms, review logic, academic power structures, and the allocation of publication rights, and further discusses the fairness of paper publication rights in the current academic dissemination system. The study argues that while preprint platforms reduce the time cost of publication in traditional journals, they have not completely eliminated publication barriers; on the contrary, they have, to some extent, created a new "soft censorship structure." Therefore, it is necessary to re-examine the governance mechanisms and academic evaluation systems of preprint platforms to promote a more equitable and transparent academic publication environment.

title: Structuring the Space of Sociotechnical Alignment: A Specification Framework and Systematic Literature Review
abstract: Sociotechnical alignment concerns the social desirability of AI behavior and is thus inherently normative, not merely technical. While NLP research increasingly addresses its technical aspects, it often leaves unspecified what such “social desirability” entails. We argue that this reflects a fundamental gap: the absence of a systematic way to specify how sociotechnical alignment defines, justifies, and evaluates socially desirable AI behavior. To address this gap, we introduce a human-centered framework for specifying sociotechnical alignment. We draw on social-scientific accounts of sociobehavioral desirability to ground the basis for behavioral desirability judgments and use this framework to analyze how alignment is specified in practice. Our systematic literature review identifies recurring patterns: normative concepts grounding desirability judgments are often unspecified or conflated with alignment targets for (desired) system behavior, target populations are underdefined, and design choices are rarely theoretically justified. These findings point to a lack of conceptual specificity that limits cumulative progress. We therefore offer recommendations that link social-scientific frameworks to alignment design choices, supporting more conceptually precise approaches to sociotechnical alignment.

title: Reputation of Punishers Depends on the Situation: Sanction Intensity and Interaction Type
abstract: Punishment is considered crucial for maintaining large-scale cooperation, but its sustainability depends on whether punishers receive reputational gains. However, previous studies have reported inconsistent findings on the reputational consequences of punishment. This study systematically examined how punishers were recognized in terms of cooperation and competitiveness. We manipulated the type of intervention (punishment of non-cooperators and compensation for victims), the intensity (the fee-to-fine ratio), and whether punishers were victims or not. Participants evaluated punishers, compensators, and non-interveners in hypothetical third-party punishment (Study 1: N = 394) and social dilemma games (Study 2: N = 400) using web-based surveys in Japan. Across both studies, punishers were perceived as more competitive. In third-party punishment, lower-intensity punishment (restoring unfair outcomes) led to reduced perceptions of competitiveness. In contrast, in social dilemma contexts (i.e., where punishers were the victims), punishers were rated as more competitive and less cooperative than non-punishers, regardless of intensity. These results suggest that the reputational consequences of punishment depend strongly on situations, and punishers potentially gain a reputation as strong individuals rather than cooperative partners.

title: Revisiting Polarization in Brazil: Evidence from Public Opinion Data
abstract: The notion of polarization has become a key lens for understanding Brazilian politics, particularly after Jair Bolsonaro’s 2018 presidential victory and his subsequent four-year administration. Many researchers maintain that Brazil is experiencing a rise in polarization, often described as either firmly ideological or as a growing preference for emotionally charged choices rather than rational ones. Some recent scholarship goes further, suggesting that polarization in the country has taken on a social dimension, with supporters of Bolsonaro and Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva increasingly avoiding direct contact with political opponents. This study critically examines such portrayals of extreme polarization. Drawing on data from the 2022 Brazilian Electoral Study (ESEB), it employs logistic regression with predicted probabilities, Multiple Correspondence Analysis (MCA), and k-modes cluster analysis to investigate how attitudes, values, policy preferences, and demographic factors shape political divisions in Brazil. The optimal number of clusters was determined through both the Elbow method and the Gap Statistic, applied consistently using the k-modes dissimilarity criterion. The findings indicate that, while certain policy issues do generate disagreement, broader differences in values and policy stances between Bolsonaro and Lula supporters are more moderate than often assumed. The four-cluster solution reveals a nuanced structure of political opinion that does not conform to a simple bipolar model. Instead of an entrenched form of polarization defined by mutual avoidance and emotional hostility, the data suggest more measured and multidimensional political differences, with asymmetry strongly favoring the right.

title: Beyond Individual Resilience: Institutional Stressors, Burnout, and Coping among Baptist Pastors in Urban Ghana
authors: McFaustine Kofi Mahama, Yaw Adu-Gyamfi, Gabriel Osei Forkuo
abstract: Background: Baptist pastors in Ghana face intense urban pressures, yet empirical studies on their occupational wellbeing are scarce. This research investigates burnout and institutional conflict, utilizing a novel socio ecological framework to uniquely link macro environmental pressures with denominational policy. Methods: A cross sectional survey instrument was administered to 200 ordained GBC pastors in Kumasi. Using Python, descriptive statistics, Pearson and Spearman correlations, Cronbach alpha reliability, Kruskal Wallis, and chi square analyses were computed alongside qualitative content analysis. A novel four level conceptual framework mapped distal context, proximal stressors, and coping strategies. Results: Church conflict was highest rated (M = 3.51, SD = 1.00; 71.5% agreement), followed by training gaps (64.5%) and work life imbalance (63.5%). Qualitative findings highlighted financial pressure, burnout, and family disruption. Mid career pastors (11-20 years) exhibited highest burnout, which did not differ significantly by location. Sabbaticals (55.5%), training (53.5%), prayer and fasting (52.0%), peer support (50.5%), and professional counselling (44.5%) were common coping strategies. Conclusions: The novel framework reveals that pastoral distress is a systemic crisis driven by institutional deficits rather than individual vulnerability. Distal urban pressures interact with proximal stressors to generate distributed distress, which is only partially moderated by coping resources. By conceptualizing these dynamic, nested connections, the framework provides a comprehensive roadmap for GBC policy interventions, demonstrating that the denomination must establish funded mental health systems, reform mandatory transfer policies, and implement structured continuing education pathways to proactively address early career vulnerability and ensure long term ministerial health across sub Saharan Africa.

title: Newly Minted Indo-Pacific Politics’ Challenges to Decolonising Taiwan’s Foreign Relations and Knowledge Production
abstract: As Taiwan is one of the hotspots of Indo-Pacific politics, its governments, political elites and intellectuals are driving the trend to make Taiwan relevant to the Indo-Pacific. The question here is not whether Taiwan should be part of the Indo-Pacific. Taiwan has no choice. Nevertheless, this article illuminates the difficulties that emerge from the interlocking of realpolitik with the production of knowledge, along with the ensuing intersections among Taiwan studies, Indo-Pacific studies, Southeast Asian studies, Austronesian studies, Indigenous studies, and studies of new immigrants as well as relevant imaginaries. These challenges are complex and multi-faceted. This article extends a modest invitation for dialogue and ongoing contemplation regarding this topic. It proposes policy advice with examples to help Taiwanese policymakers, political elites, and educators understand how to incorporate decolonial thinking into their future planning related to the knowledge realm of the Indo-Pacific.

title: When Intercultural Experience Sharpens the Palate: Food Culture, Disgust, and Acceptability Judgements in Chinese Adults
authors: Roberto Filippi, Liang Hu, Olivia Yang, Lam Yuen Sun, Yinuo Tian, Tong Wu, Paola Bonifacci, Li Wei
abstract: Food preferences and acceptability judgements are shaped by cultural experience, yet little is known about how transnational experience influences responses to culturally defined food norm violations. This study examined Chinese adults’ affective and evaluative responses to culturally appropriate and norm-violating food pairings from Chinese and Italian culinary contexts. One hundred and sixty-five Chinese adults completed a Culinary Violation Task, rating each pairing for disgust and appropriateness. Participants also reported whether they had lived abroad and completed a measure of dispositional disgust sensitivity. Trial-level mixed-effects models showed that norm-violating items elicited stronger disgust and lower appropriateness ratings than culturally appropriate items, with the violation effect substantially stronger for Chinese than Italian food items. Overseas experience was associated with heightened disgust responses to norm-violating items, especially Chinese food norm violations, while appropriateness judgements showed broader sensitivity to norm structure across both food cultures. Disgust sensitivity predicted stronger affective responses but did not account for the main overseas-experience effect. These findings challenge a simple exposure-as-openness account and suggest that transnational experience may heighten sensitivity to culturally patterned culinary norms, particularly when violations concern heritage food culture.

title: Economic Impact of Global Warming in India: An Updated Review of Methods, Policies, and Recent Empirical Literature*
abstract: This Chapter reviews empirical methodologies studying the impact of global warming on economic indicators inIndia. We begin with an analysis of global climate dynamics, which includes definitional debate of level and growtheffects, using climate econometrics to highlight the methodological issue in temperature-GDP econometrics, andsummarising methodological discussion on differentiation between weather and climate change. The chapter thenevaluates India's vulnerability to climate shocks, detailing macroeconomic impacts on critical sectors such asagriculture, health, and productivity, and reviews the evolution of India's environmental policies. We further reviewthe role of fiscal, regulatory, and monetary policies in facilitating climate adaptation and mitigation. Additionally,we explore diverse econometric approaches for estimating climate change impacts, including cross-sections andpanel methods. This review highlights the urgency of coordinated international action and the need for India toimplement robust climate policies to ensure sustainable economic growth and fulfil its emission reduction targets.

title: Ecological Sustainability and Indigenous Knowledge Systems in Textile Dyeing: The Dabu Mud-Resist Architecture of SA Fab in Bagru
abstract: The transition toward sustainable manufacturing in the global textile industry necessitates a re-evaluation of indigenous, zero-chemical production frameworks. This paper analyzes the ancient "Dabu" mud-resist block printing process utilized by the Chhipa artisan community of Bagru, Rajasthan. Acting as a primary case study, the preservation efforts and operational architecture of SA Fab demonstrate how 450-year-old botanical and geological methodologies offer a scalable alternative to ecologically damaging synthetic textile dyeing. By deconstructing the chemical composition of the mud-resist paste (kali mitti, chuna, gond, and beedhan) and the natural indigo vat dyeing protocol, this paper formalizes the Dabu technique into the academic record as a vital framework for contemporary sustainable fashion.

title: One Gradient, Not Two Poles: The Asymmetric Structure of Violence Tolerance in the Brazilian Electorate
abstract: Does the ideological cleavage that organizes Brazilian politics also structure citizens’ tolerance of violence, and does it do so symmetrically? Drawing on the August 2023 wave of the national survey A Cara da Democracia (N = 2,558), this article treats tolerance of violence as a multidimensional construct — lethal punitivism, tolerance of authoritarian rupture, and approval of insurrectionary violence — and tests how three operationalizations of the political cleavage (the 2022 second-round vote, left–right self-placement, and partisan identity) predict each. Dimensionality diagnostics show that the coup-justification items form a strong unidimensional scale while the punitivism items do not cohere, a substantive result that disciplines the modeling. Weighted logistic models yield three findings. First, the cleavage is large and graded, concentrated on tolerance of authoritarian rupture: a Bolsonaro voter is roughly five times as likely as a Lula voter to justify a coup, net of controls. Second, the cleavage is asymmetric rather than a symmetric polarization: entering antipetismo and antibolsonarismo jointly, only rejection of the Workers’ Party predicts greater tolerance of violence, while rejection of Bolsonaro’s party is mildly protective; a formal test rejects their equality on every endpoint. Third, the gap widens with education and income — a pattern of the comfortable rather than the deprived. A multiple correspondence analysis recovers a single violence-endorsement gradient onto which the cleavage maps asymmetrically. Crime salience neither predicts punitivism nor amplifies the vote effect. The Brazilian case suggests that the mass-attitudinal substrate of democratic backsliding is organized by political-identity alignment, not by fear of crime, and that it is asymmetric by construction.

title: Open K-Anonymized Aggregates from a Free Personal-Finance Calculator Platform: Methodology, Dataset, and Public Mirror
abstract: We present CalcFi, an open infrastructure for citation-backed consumer-finance calculators built on US primary economic and tax sources. Every calculator on calcfi.app derives its inputs from a closed whitelist of primary sources (IRS, BLS, BEA, FRED, U.S. Treasury, CFPB, HUD, U.S. Census Bureau, SSA, Freddie Mac PMMS) with provenance enforced via a SourcedValue type contract, retrievedAt timestamps, and stale-data floors. We document the methodology, the data layer's exposure via open REST APIs, an MCP server distributed on npm and PyPI, llms.txt for LLM-discovery, and the archival flow into Zenodo with citable per-snapshot DOIs. We argue consumer-finance calculators should ship audit trails at parity with academic data citations, and demonstrate a reproducibility framework that enables third-party replication of every numeric output. CC-BY 4.0.

title: Village Productivity Index (VPI) / Indeks Produktivitas Desa (IPD): A New Framework for Measuring Rural Economic Contribution Beyond Administrative Performance
abstract: Indonesia channels more than IDR 70 trillion annually through village fiscal transfers to 75,265 village governments without a nationally standardized instrument for measuring the economic productivity of recipient units. Existing performance indices (the Village Development Index (IDM), SDGs Desa, and Indeks Desa) evaluate administrative compliance and service delivery; none measures village economic output. This paper introduces the Village Productivity Index (VPI/IPD), a composite index operationalizing the productive territorial unit (PTU) framework to assess rural economic productivity across five weighted dimensions: primary sector output (25%), economic diversification (20%), labor productivity (20%), Dana Desa fiscal return (20%), and market integration (15%). Theoretically, the VPI integrates Pritchett, Woolcock, and Andrews’s (2013) isomorphic mimicry framework with North’s (1990) institutional economics and OECD Rural Policy 3.0. Methodologically, the index employs Winsorized normalization at national percentiles and a three-year backward-moving average for the fiscal return dimension to address outlier sensitivity and capital circulation lag. A stratified pilot empirical analysis on 1,200 villages across six provinces demonstrates meaningful inter-village discrimination (mean VPI = 51.3, SD = 14.7; range 18.4–82.6) and preliminary predictive validity with district PDRB per capita. Monte Carlo sensitivity analysis confirms classification stability for 78.4% of villages across plausible weight variations. The comparative analysis positions the VPI within the landscape of rural development indicators, addressing a key gap in existing administrative frameworks. Integration of VPI into Dana Desa allocation formulae under UU HKPD No. 1 Tahun 2022 is simulated to assess potential resource redirection and aggregate economic outcomes. Keywords: Village Productivity Index; Indeks Produktivitas Desa; rural economic productivity; productive territorial unit; Dana Desa; isomorphic mimicry; fiscal transfer; Indonesia village policy; rural value added; composite index methodology

title: Patient Perceptions of Artificial Intelligence in Suicide Risk Assessment Models: Insights from Three Tribal Communities
authors: Valentín Quiroz de la Sierra, Rohan Dayal, Paul Rebman, Novalene Goklish, Hannah Metwally, Pua Yang, Kristin Mitchell, Timothy Werwie, Renae Begay, Roy Adams
abstract: Suicide rates in American Indian and Alaska Native (AIAN) communities are nearly double the US national average. Artificial Intelligence (AI)-assisted suicide risk identification tools offer potential for early detection, but their cultural acceptability and ethical implications in healthcare settings that serve AIAN populations remain underexplored. This study examines patient perspectives on existing suicide screening practices, stigma, trust, patient-provider communication, and AI-assisted suicide prevention in Tribal-serving healthcare settings. A qualitative study was conducted in three Tribal communities in the Southwestern US involving 25 AIAN adults who had received care at a Tribal-serving healthcare facility in the past two years. Semi-structured interviews explored experiences with existing suicide screening, barriers to care-seeking, and perceptions of AI-assisted suicide risk identification tools. Inductive content analysis identified key barriers and facilitators. Stigma remains a primary barrier to seeking care as exacerbated by distrust in the healthcare system. Existing suicide screening practices were perceived as impersonal, which discouraged disclosure. Participants emphasized the need for culturally responsive, strengths-based communication to foster trust. AI-assisted risk identification elicited mixed reactions; while some saw potential benefits, others expressed concerns about privacy, risk of misdiagnosis, and the potential for long-term stigma. Culturally grounded suicide prevention efforts must prioritize trust-building, Tribal governance over AI-assisted tools, and healthcare provider training in patient-centered communication. AI technology holds promise but must be developed under Tribal governance and with community leadership to ensure ethical implementation. With community guidance, AI-assisted tools can complement existing prevention efforts and expand access to culturally responsive care.

title: How Young Adults Prioritize Public Values When Choosing Mental Wellness Apps: A Mixed-Methods Study
authors: Tonka Milošević, Marilisa Boffo, Julia van Weert, Esther Rozendaal
abstract: Background: Mental wellness apps (MWAs) are increasingly used by young adults as demand for mental health support grows. The rapid adoption of AI in MWAs creates opportunities for more personalized care but also raises risks, including reduced user autonomy and sensitive data privacy concerns. Since MWAs are classified as “general wellness” tools, they fall outside the EU's Medical Devices Regulation, potentially leaving key public values unprotected. However, little is known about how a vulnerable subgroup of young adults, university students, assess or adopt MWAs, or how they weigh different public values in their decisions. Methods: A mixed-methods approach was employed combining semi-structured interviews and a hierarchical card sorting task. University students at a Dutch university (N=27) were interviewed on the role of 10 public values (autonomy, authenticity, agency, fairness, integrity, solidarity, privacy, security, trustworthiness, and transparency) in the decision-making process behind choosing to adopt an MWA. Additionally, they ranked these 10 values on importance in this process. Results: Reflexive thematic analysis yielded five themes: (1) motives for private MWA use, (2) tensions between digital resignation and privacy concerns, (3) how severity and urgency of mental health needs shape value trade-offs, (4) perceived personal benefits or drawbacks of specific values, and (5) sources of trust when adopting an MWA. By-person factor analysis of value rankings revealed four prioritization patterns centered on: (1) data protection, (2) effectiveness, (3) secure and reliable outcomes, or (4) fairness. No consensus in value priorities emerged. Discussion and conclusions: Although MWAs are unregulated, students are expected to make informed choices and bear the responsibility of consequences. Yet, their value priorities differ depending on personal circumstances, making some more exposed to risks than others. Many feel resigned and unable to influence how MWAs handle the values they care about, leading them to downplay those values, assuming apps will not realize them anyway. The findings show concrete mechanisms through which harm can emerge, especially considering most students turn to MWAs mainly in times of urgent need. This further emphasizes the need for regulation to ensure MWA safety, quality, and protection of public values, particularly as AI features continue to expand. Keywords: mental wellness apps, mHealth, mental health, university students, value trade-offs, public values

title: Will a Rising Middle Class in the Global South Reshape Politics?
abstract: Discussions of the “global middle class” in the early 21st Century argue that this group expanded in the Global South from 1990-2010 as a product of economic growth. This discussion echoes the 1960s debate regarding the relationship between economic development and democracy. This entry focuses on the economic structure of middle classes in the Global South, their role in housing and consumption markets as it influences middle class urban politics, and the role of middle classes in political change in the Global South. Middle classes have grown in the Global South, though their economic characteristics are diverse and their members are at risk of downward mobility due to precarious employment patterns and periodic economic shocks. The expansion of gated communities and shopping malls in cities of the Global South has generated patterns of cross-class segregation that leads members of the middle classes to engage in exclusionary practices toward the poor. However, some members of the middle classes engage in progressive urban social movements. While scholars in the 1960s were optimistic that a growing middle class would encourage democratization, subsequent scholarship has shown that middle classes display inconsistent support for democracy and engage in diverse forms of political expression. These expressions include both participation in formal politics and engagement with social movements. Additionally, middle classes are internally divided based on religious affiliation, racial and ethnic identities, birth cohort membership, and occupation, leading to competing political identities across middle class subgroups. This diversity suggests that efforts to predict how the middle classes may reshape politics should attend to national historical and political legacies to understand the distinctive pathways of middle-class politics in the Global South.

title: Crime and Symbolic Boundaries in Unsettled Times: An Analysis of Middle-Class Responses to Fear in Chile
abstract: Students of crime in the U.S. examine drug economies in poor communities (Bourgois 1995; Contreras 2013), poor residents’ adaptive behaviors (Kirk and Matsuda 2011; Sampson, Raudenbush, and Earls 1997) and punitive crime policies (Garland 2023; Picket 2019; Wacquant 2010). Research on Latin America, the most violent region in the world (Vilalta 2020), focuses on criminal governance (Feldmann and Luna 2022), violence in poor communities (Auyero and Sobering 2019), and the popularity of punitive policies (Chouhy, Singer, and Lehmann 2022). However, few scholars have examined middle class residents’ experiences of crime. This article builds on work on middle classes (Caldeira 2000; Villareal 2025), and utilizes interviews and observations in three Santiago, Chile communities. Findings focus on middle-class Chileans’ interpretations of crime, adaptive behaviors, and analyses of crime policies. In unsettled times (Swidler 1986), individuals construct symbolic boundaries (Lamont 1992, 2000, 2018) with criminals and their peers. Direct experiences with crime as well as cultural and ideological beliefs shape middle class individuals’ contrasting interpretations of crime and construction of boundaries with others. These divergent attitudes reflect enduring fissures within Chilean society over its dictatorship (1973-1990) and recent social upheaval (protests, immigration, and rising crime), complicating universalizing narratives about a punitive Chilean citizenry. Keywords: crime,

title: Digital Platform Work Beyond the Workplace: Household Risk and Welfare Inaccessibility among Delivery Rider Families in China
abstract: Digital platform work is usually analysed through algorithmic control, flexible contracting and risk transfer between platforms and workers. This study extends this debate beyond the workplace by examining how platform-mediated labour insecurity becomes household precarity and welfare inaccessibility. Drawing on a qualitative multiple-case study of 22 delivery-rider households in Xiamen, China, together with anonymised service case records from 10 community-based support cases, the analysis shows how app-based coordination, data-mediated income visibility, platform-account use and ambiguous employment relations shape family decisions about rent, debt, illness, schooling and care. Spouses, children and older parents absorb platform-related risks by adjusting employment, care routines and settlement plans, while fragmented welfare rules often turn work insecurity into administrative burden. The study contributes to scholarship on digital labour, employment relations and social protection by showing how technology-mediated labour insecurity is reproduced through household relations and institutional boundaries.

title: Rethinking Development: From Growth to Livelihoodbility
abstract: Few would dispute the importance of development, however it is defined or interpreted. It speaks not only to policy or academic inquiry, but to a broader civilisational aspiration: the enduring human effort to improve the material, social, and moral conditions of life across generations. Precisely because of this centrality, development has become one of the most prominent ideas in human thought and practice. Yet it is also among the most contested, functioning variously as aspiration, ideology, analytical framework, policy objective, and moral project. While everyday discourse often assumes a shared understanding, scholarly engagement reveals a concept marked by plurality, tension, and continual reinterpretation. Drawing on extensive desk research, qualitative survey evidence, and a case study, this volume brings together diverse strands of development thinking into a coherent analytical whole. Building on insights into shifting meanings of development and evolving development narratives, it introduces the concept of “livelihoodbility” (derived from livelihoods and liveability), advancing a working definition of development that seeks to bridge normative aspiration with analytical measurability. It further organises development thinking into three intersecting axes, expressed through the “development cube,” an orthogonal framework that functions both as a conceptual model and a practical tool. This framework allows development to be examined across contexts, disciplines, and epistemic traditions, and offers potential application in the empirical assessment of policies, programmes, and outcomes. Anchored in a China-focused case study, the work argues against privileging any single theoretical lens. Instead, it calls for the strategic integration of multiple perspectives in designing context-sensitive policies and programmes. Finally, the study raises difficult but necessary questions about humanity’s broader developmental trajectory and the possibility of a civilisational impasse. These are not posed to suggest inevitability or decline, but to encourage critical reflection on the future of development. If development remains a central human aspiration, its future depends not on rhetorical affirmation, but on intellectual clarity, ethical commitment, and collective yet differentiated responsibility.

title: The Impact of Servant Leadership, Peer Support, and Psychological Safety on Employee Burnout in the Public Sector
authors: Thomas Brandon Fields, Maranda Taylor, Mackinsey Flowers, Linda K. Hash, Danielle Costanza, Sabrina Demsky-Cameron, Francesca Bianco
abstract: Employee burnout is characterized by emotional exhaustion, depersonalization, and a diminished sense of personal accomplishment. This has become a common workplace issue, with a high prevalence in helping fields such as healthcare, mental health, and social work organizations. Past research has explored how servant leadership, peer support, and psychological safety impact employee burnout. However, there is still a significant gap in understanding how these factors interact to manage employee burnout. While each factor independently can contribute to healthier work environments, this study shows the impact of these factors when combined to address employee burnout. Peer support was found to reduce exhaustion and depersonalization and increase feelings of personal achievement. Psychological safety was found to decrease exhaustion and increase feelings of personal achievement. Aspects of servant leadership were also found to reduce exhaustion and depersonalization and increase feelings of personal achievement. Our findings also show that components of servant leadership increased exhaustion and depersonalization, and decreased feelings of personal achievement. Further, psychological safety was found to increase exhaustion among some employees. This study addresses evidence-based practice gaps by synthesizing findings to guide organizations in developing reasonable strategies to improve their work culture and reduce employee burnout.

title: How Leadership Style Relates to Pluralistic Ignorance About Misinformation in the Workplace
authors: Gabor Orosz, Laura Farago, Janos Salamon, Steven J. Spencer
abstract: Pluralistic ignorance—misperceiving others as more supportive of a norm or belief than oneself—can facilitate the spread of misinformation in hierarchical settings. In a cross-sectional study of employed adults (N = 402), we examined whether perceived leadership style is associated with such misperceptions when misinformation is shared by a supervisor. Participants rated how likely they themselves, and how likely their coworkers, would be to openly agree with, hide disagreement about, or genuinely believe fake-news headlines endorsed by their boss. Across all three outcomes, participants judged their coworkers to be more compliant with the boss’s misinformation than they reported themselves to be, consistent with a self–other discrepancy indicative of pluralistic ignorance. The size of this discrepancy varied with perceived leadership style: it was larger under authoritarian and laissez-faire leadership than under autonomy-supportive leadership, and laissez-faire leadership was associated with the largest discrepancies in perceived coworker compliance and concealment. Because the design is cross-sectional and based on hypothetical scenarios, these associations are correlational and several involve small effects. The findings are consistent with the possibility that leadership climates marked by uncertainty or low psychological safety distort perceived social norms surrounding misinformation, with potential implications for organizational communication and employee silence.

title: Your Concerns Have Been Noted: Constituent Influence and Support for Housing
abstract: Opposition to new housing development is widespread and neighbourhood defenders— proximate residents who mobilize against proposed projects—are a well-documented feature of local planning politics. Yet we know relatively little about how political elites process these place-based claims. Using a conjoint experiment embedded in the 2026 Canadian Municipal Barometer, I examine the extent to which city councilors weight the preferences of constituents based on their proximity to a proposed housing development. The results confirm that councilors are more responsive to proximate con stituents. This proximity effect is amplified by neighbourhood tenure and is stronger for denser development types. Contrary to expectations, neither the councilor’s ideol ogy, density preferences, electoral district type, nor acclamation status conditions this responsiveness—suggesting that localism is a structural feature of local representation rather than an ideological predisposition. Taken together, the findings suggest that place-protective claims find a receptive audience among political elites.

title: From Status Symbol to Polysemous Sign: Interpreting the Meaning of Artificially Extended Fingernails
abstract: The study analyses how German-language press coverage interprets the trend toward artificially elongated fingernails and relates it to Veblen’s concept of conspicuous leisure.
