Unlike temporal work, which preserves future horizons, moral adaptation relocated moral evaluation away from anticipated outcomes and toward present conduct, redefining the meanings of strategy, obligation, and community, and preceding the group’s later organizational transformation. The paper theorizes moral adaptation as a mechanism of resilience distinct from identity maintenance, emotional management, and temporal work. In doing so, it advances the case for treating moral grammars as dynamic, evaluative structures whose temporal orientation can shift under adverse conditions, contributing to the integration of the sociology of morality and the sociology of temporality.

title: Factors associated with disclosure of protected characteristics: a systematic review and meta-synthesis
authors: George Wood, Rachel Connolly, David Nicholls, Jeremy Dietz
abstract: Background: Employers increasingly collect equality, diversity and inclusion data, and reducing non-disclosure rates is often used as a key performance indicator. However, why people with protected characteristics choose either to disclose or withhold this information at work has not, to our knowledge, been systematically reviewed. This review is a thematic synthesis of qualitative studies examining the experiences of disclosure in the workplace of people with protected characteristics according to UK law including disability, gender reassignment, race and sexual orientation. Methods: A grey literature search was conducted in October 2022 using Google, Lens.org and Web of Science to identify English-language systematic reviews and qualitative studies on adults’ workplace experiences of disclosing or not disclosing protected characteristics. A thematic synthesis was developed from these studies, and the search was rerun in July 2025 to identify additional evidence and update the themes. The risk of bias of each study was assessed using the CASP qualitative checklist. Results: Findings from 60 papers were included in the review, including two meta-ethnographies and a further 19 studies. The thematic synthesis yielded five main themes from the experiences and views on disclosure in the workplace from adults with protected characteristics: 1) Disclosure is an important, ongoing life event that has significant implications; 2) When people do not disclose, they may develop strategies to moderate disclosure that require energy to maintain; 3) Moderators of disclosure; 4) Positive consequences of disclosure; 5) Negative consequences of disclosure. Limitations: There was limited evidence on the experience of people with visible disabilities, bisexual people, and trans people, and no evidence discussing the experiences of people disclosing information about ethnicity. No studies were found discussing intersectionality of identities and how this might affect disclosure. Limited information was reported regarding participants' jobs, and some of the studies were older and conducted in contexts that might be less likely to reflect UK culture today. Conclusions: Employers who focus on creating a trusting, supportive environment; actively mitigating the risks of disclosure; reassuring staff about how disclosures will be handled; and meeting accessibility and adjustment needs can improve the workplace experiences of people with minority identities and potentially increase disclosure rates.

title: Real Estate Wealth Is Not a Monolith: Non-Primary Real Estate and Its Role in Wealth Concentration
authors: Pietro Valetto, Stefano Filauro, Ive Marx, Janet C. Gornick, Carolyn Fisher
abstract: Housing wealth is widely recognized as the most equally distributed major asset class in advanced economies, anchoring middle-class wealth portfolios and overall wealth inequality. Yet, in this paper we observe how treating real estate as a monolith obscures a critical divide. Disaggregating net housing wealth into primary real estate equity and non-primary real estate equity reveals different distributional patterns that fundamentally alter our understanding of housing's role in the wealth distribution. Using harmonized microdata from the EU HFCS (2021) and U.S. SCF (2022), we show that while primary residences are indeed more equally distributed than overall wealth, non-primary real estate such as vacation homes, rental properties, land, and business properties makes up the most highly concentrated component of household portfolios, surpassing even financial assets. Our Gini factor decomposition analysis across various European countries and the U.S. reveals that non-primary real estate exhibits extreme concentration, with Gini coefficients ranging from 0.88 to 0.96, and contributes disproportionately to overall inequality relative to its wealth share. Therefore, the equalizing effect of housing relative to other asset classes emphasized in prior research holds only for owner-occupied homes. These findings suggest that policies treating all housing uniformly may inadvertently amplify wealth concentration, particularly in Europe where non-primary property ownership is nearly twice as prevalent as in the United States. (Stone Center Working Paper Series)

title: Can Nonprofits Survive their Founders? Assessing Succession Effects in Private Museums
abstract: Executive succession has widely been seen as disruptive for nonprofit organizations, negatively affecting performance in general and survival in particular. However, quantitative research on the succession-survival link in nonprofit organizations is limited, and findings are mixed. To extend knowledge in this area, a study on succession effects is conducted for a population of private art museums. These arts organizations show heavy founder involvement in funding, governance and vision, and have hence been argued to be vulnerable to executive succession. Succession effects in event-history analysis (368 organizations, 4371 organization-years) are only moderate and insignificant, yet might be more detrimental in some cases, such as for organizations that are small and opened by male founders. It is argued that considering the contingencies of organizational succession is required to comprehensively assess its hazards and opportunities.

title: Stalled or distorted? Tempo Effects in Africa’s Fertility Transition
abstract: Fertility rates in Africa have remained high, declined slowly, and stalled before reaching replacement level. However, there is little consensus on which countries truly stalled as existing research uses a variety of competing methods that are disconnected from fertility theory, do not account for tempo effects in the Total Fertility Rate, and rely too heavily on subjective researcher judgment. This study revisits fertility stalls using the first and second derivatives of tempo-adjusted TFR and a piecewise regression approach. This method reveals four previously unidentified stalls in Sudan, Botswana, Mauritania, and South Africa, as well as nine further stalls in other countries. Stalls in Botswana, South Africa, and Zimbabwe are particularly severe. While tempo effects do not fully explain stalls, they are positively associated, suggesting that stalls may partially reflect changes in the timing rather than total amount of fertility.

title: Sanctioned Scholars: The Price Of Speaking Freely In Today’s Academy
abstract: The Foundation for Individual Rights and Expression (FIRE), a nonprofit organization committed to defending and sustaining the individual rights of all Americans to free speech and free thought, maintains a database documenting the ways and reasons scholars have faced calls for sanction because of their speech. This report presents the results of a survey of 209 scholars in this Scholars Under Fire database who were targeted for sanction because of their speech between 2020 and 2024.

title: Relational Wealth: Ownership Diffusion and Ownership Confusion in Wealthy Families
abstract: Economic elites have accumulated staggering resources. But scholars tend to conceptualize their wealth as singularly owned by individuals or households, neglecting long-established relational aspects of economic behavior. Using 30 interviews and six months of in-depth ethnographic observation at a “family office” that manages large fortunes, I ask how relationships shape elite perceptions of wealth ownership and the subsequent flows of family wealth. I find that when assets are legally shared (“diffused”), ownership perceptions can mismatch, and ownership can be “confused”. When owner- ship is confused, diffusing actors like family offices can influence ownership perceptions, shaping how elites wield wealth in practice. These shifts usually, but not exclusively, benefit “patriarchs.” Understanding the increasingly consequential resources of economic elites requires us to examine the assumption of unambiguous ownership: elite wealth is fundamentally entangled in familial and professional relationships.

title: ROI and the Misclassification of Criminal Justice Education Programs: How Accountability Metrics Shape Public Universities and the Evaluation of CJ Education
abstract: Criminal Justice (CJ) education programs at public universities are increasingly evaluated through state level ROI frameworks that tie program value to short term earnings and workforce alignment. This article shows how these models, now embedded in many funding and accountability regimes, tend to misclassify CJ education programs. Descriptive data from the National Center for O*NET Development, BLS, College Scorecard, and state salary databases reveal three key structural mismatches: most CJ occupations do not require a bachelor’s degree; public sector wage structures depress early career earnings; and ROI classifications shift with occupational and temporal definitions. As a result, ROI models capture labor market composition and state funding priorities rather than program value, obscuring CJ’s civic and community roles.

title: CONVERGENT FAULTS : A Quantitative Forensic of Private Credit's Synchronized Systemic Risk (2026–2027)
abstract: Private credit has gone from roughly $400 billion in 2009 to about $3 trillion in mid-2026. U.S. business development companies (BDCs) now hold close to half a trillion of that, split across three architectures with very different liquidity and disclosure properties. The official line, repeated by the Federal Reserve, the Financial Stability Board and most sell-side credit desks, calls the resulting risk "limited and manageable." Fund-level leverage is low, defaults are below the long-run leveraged-loan average, and bank exposure looks bounded. We argue that the official line works only when each stress channel is examined alone, and breaks down once the channels are read together. This paper documents five channels: software and SaaS valuation compression under AI disruption; bank-to-non-bank credit tightening, with JPMorgan as first mover; retail liquidity gating at perpetual non-traded BDCs; the "Bermuda Triangle" of related-party lending between Apollo and Athene, KKR and Global Atlantic, Brookfield and its affiliated insurers; and the 2026–2027 refinancing wall hitting a sector that has just lost its bid. Using Bloomberg Intelligence proprietary research, regulatory data and the public filings of the largest BDCs, we show that these channels are coupled. Each one tightens the others under plausible macroeconomic conditions, and the joint stress horizon is short enough to read on a calendar. The cascade is already visible. On 4 June 2026 Blackstone Private Credit Fund, the largest BDC, $82.1 billion of holdings, gated quarterly redemptions at 5% of NAV against requests of 10%. Its $1 billion 6% 2032 notes trade 150 basis points wide of triple-B financial peers, against an adviser (Blackstone Inc.) rated A+/A+. We map the catalysts that lie ahead, assign probabilities to three outcome scenarios, and identify a convergence window centred on Q1–Q2 2027.

title: Broad Support for Democratic Erosion: The Case of Trump’s TSA Order
abstract: Public opinion surveys consistently find high support for democracy in the abstract. Studying the case of a popular but unconstitutional executive order, this note shows that broad, cross-partisan support for democratic erosion can emerge when it is attached to a popular policy goal. In March 2026, U.S. president Donald Trump ordered government to pay Transportation Security Administration (TSA) agents despite the lack of a congressional appropriation, in plain violation of Article I, Section 9 of the U.S. Constitution. About two-thirds of Americans approved of the order, including 59% of Democrats and 2024 Kamala Harris voters. Approval was substantially higher among those who correctly answered a question about which branch of government decides how money is spent under the Constitution. The findings suggest that episodes of executive aggrandizement can enjoy broad support when they are attached to popular policy goals, and that civic knowledge does not necessarily act as a bulwark against such support.

title: Ethically Informed Algorithmic Matching and Refugee Resettlement
authors: Ahmed Ezzeldin Mohamed, Craig Damian Smith
abstract: This article discusses real-world projects using algorithms to match resettled refugees with sponsors and services. It argues that ethically informed algorithmic matching can support larger-scale and better-informed resettlement while avoiding simplistic techno-sceptical or techno-optimistic framings. The article emphasizes the dual-use function of technology in international mobility and the importance of ethical design.

title: Fathers and menstruation: a scoping review
authors: Lavanya Gera, Danielle Jane Russell, Dani Barrington
abstract: Introduction: Despite the well-established influence of family environments on menstrual health, existing research overwhelmingly centres mothers, leaving fathers’ contributions insufficiently understood. Yet Fathers have the potential to play an influential role in their daughters’ menstrual experiences. This review synthesises qualitative evidence on paternal involvement globally, using Lamb’s triadic father involvement framework (accessibility, engagement, and responsibility) to understand how fathers participate in, respond to, or are excluded from menstrual related interactions. Methods: A scoping review of qualitative or qualitative components of mixed-methods peer-reviewed studies published after 2000 was conducted. Studies were compiled through a four-stage search strategy followed by screening. Thematic synthesis was achieved through deductive coding using Lamb’s framework and inductive identification of contextual influences. Results: 105 studies met the inclusion criteria. Fathers demonstrated both positive and negative accessibility and engagement, particularly in relation to provision of menstrual products and willingness to discuss menstrual issues. Positive responsibility was evident in facilitating healthcare, though findings were limited. Three inductive themes; menstrual concealment, perceived lack of credibility, and maternal gatekeeping shaped father involvement across the three domains, often undermining fathers’ willingness and capacity to support daughters, despite expressing concern about their daughters’ menstrual experience. Conclusion: Fathers’ involvement in menstrual experiences is shaped by paternal knowledge gaps regarding menstruation, gendered norms, and family dynamics that promote menstrual concealment. Evidence remains fragmented and rarely explores the drivers of paternal behaviour. Findings highlight opportunities for father-inclusive education, stigma-reduction, and structural change, and underscore the need for research across diverse cultural contexts.

title: El ciclo de las Políticas Sociales
abstract: Documento de carácter académico y docente que analiza el ciclo de vida de las políticas sociales como marco para comprender cómo se construyen, aplican, evalúan y reformulan las respuestas públicas ante los problemas sociales. El texto presenta las cuatro etapas principales del ciclo —diagnóstico, diseño, implementación y evaluación—, explicando sus funciones, elementos clave y ejemplos aplicados al contexto español. Asimismo, se reflexiona sobre factores transversales que condicionan todo el proceso, como la relación entre agenda pública y agenda gubernamental, la participación social, la sostenibilidad institucional y el equilibrio entre estandarización y flexibilidad. Se trata, en suma, de un material de síntesis orientado a la comprensión teórica y a la práctica profesional.

title: LA FORMACIÓN CRÍTICA DEL PSICÓLOGO. DERECHOS HUMANOS Y ÉTICA PROFESIONAL.
abstract: Este trabajo propone reflexionar críticamente acerca del rol de la Universidad, en relación a su principal deber y función consagrado en la formación académica. Se analiza como problemática actual el déficit o ausencia de ciertos contenidos claves en la formación universitaria, particularmente del psicólogo/a. Vinculando dicha problemática al cuestionamiento de la obsolescencia del proceso educativo universitario. Repensando dicho proceso como una práctica compleja y dialéctica, que debe ser abordada de forma tal que responda a las necesidades de la sociedad. Partiendo desde la perspectiva de autonomía en el proceso de aprendizaje activo, entendido como modelo deseable. En función de la eficacia e impacto social de la Universidad, se expone la creciente necesidad del tratamiento explícito, obligatorio y constante de la cuestión Derechos Humanos y Ética. Entendido como contenido curricular que no puede faltar en la formación de un profesional crítico, capacitado y comprometido con la sociedad y sus problemáticas actuales. Si bien gran parte del análisis se centra en la Facultad de psicología de la UNMDP, se entiende que el debate excede la especificidad de nuestra carrera y puede extenderse a otras carreras de acuerdo a sus particularidades y singularidades. This work proposes to critically reflect on the role of the University regarding its main duty and function enshrined in academic training. The deficit or absence of certain key contents in university education, particularly for psychologists, is analyzed as a current problem. This issue is linked to questioning the obsolescence of the university educational process, rethinking said process as a complex and dialectical practice that must be approached in such a way that it responds to the needs of society. Starting from the perspective of autonomy in the active learning process, understood as a desirable model, and based on the efficacy and social impact of the University, the growing need for the explicit, mandatory, and constant treatment of Human Rights and Ethics is presented. This is understood as a curricular content that cannot be missing from the training of a critical, qualified professional committed to society and its current problems. Although a large part of the analysis focuses on the Faculty of Psychology at the UNMDP, it is understood that the debate exceeds the specificity of our program and can be extended to other programs according to their particularities and singularities.

title: LA FANTASÍA EN LA PRÁCTICA EDUCATIVA COMO OBSTÁCULO EN LA FORMACIÓN CRÍTICA DEL PSICÓLOGO. DERECHOS HUMANOS, VALORES Y ÉTICA.
abstract: Este trabajo presenta un análisis crítico sobre la formación profesional del psicólogo en la universidad pública, poniendo el foco en las prácticas pedagógicas y la necesidad de una inclusión transversal de los Derechos Humanos, los Valores y la Ética profesional. A partir de una mirada transdisciplinar que articula la Pedagogía Social y la vivencia institucional en la Facultad de Psicología (UNMdP), se conceptualiza el fenómeno de "la fantasía en la práctica educativa". Este concepto busca cuestionar las prácticas actuales educativas como un dilema estructural, presenta la contradicción existente entre un discurso oficial de horizontalidad y prácticas concretas escolarizantes, pasivas y verticalistas que alienan al estudiante y reproducen la desigualdad social. Finalmente, fundamentado en la Pedagogía de la Pregunta de Paulo Freire y en el marco de la Ley de Educación Superior (Art. 43) y la Res. Ministerial 343/09, se propone desnaturalizar las lógicas universitarias actuales para transitar de una mera transmisión de conocimientos hacia una praxis comprometida, orientada a la producción de profesionales críticos y hacedores capaces de responder a las demandas sociales actuales y promover una sociedad más justa. This work presents a critical analysis of the professional training of psychologists in public universities, focusing on pedagogical practices and the need for a transversal inclusion of Human Rights, Values, and Professional Ethics. From a transdisciplinary perspective that articulates Social Pedagogy and institutional experience at the Faculty of Psychology (UNMdP), the phenomenon of "fantasy in educational practice" is conceptualized. This concept seeks to question current educational practices as a structural dilemma, presenting the contradiction between an official discourse of horizontality and concrete schooling, passive, and vertical practices that alienate students and reproduce social inequality. Finally, grounded in Paulo Freire's Pedagogy of the Question and within the framework of the Higher Education Law (Art. 43) and Ministerial Resolution 343/09, it proposes to denaturalize current university logics to move from a mere transmission of knowledge towards a committed praxis, aimed at producing critical and proactive professionals capable of responding to current social demands and promoting a more equitable society.

title: The Moral Grammar of Magnifica Humanitas - A Discussion Paper
abstract: On May 15th 2026, on the 135th anniversary of Rerum Novarum, Pope Leo XIV promulgated (published on May 25th) the encyclical Magnifica Humanitas — “On safeguarding the human person in the time of Artificial Intelligence.” Two months earlier (March 2026), the author had circulated, through SSRN and RePEc (DOI: 10.2139/ssrn.6468803), The Moral Grammar of Economics: An Empirical and Mathematical Framework for Jesuit-Inspired Social Justice. The present paper performs a systematic dialogue between the two texts: (i) it identifies the doctrinal claims of Magnifica Humanitas that admit a direct mathematical operationalization through the constraints, wedges, and welfare functions developed in the Moral Grammar; (ii) it isolates the novel doctrinal contributions introduced by Leo XIV — the Babel/Jerusalem dichotomy, the disarmament of words and code, the new monopolies of AI, the culture of care, and the explicit move beyond GDP — and formalises each as new terms or constraints within the welfare architecture; (iii) it re-anchors these formal extensions in the panel-data evidence already presented in the Moral Grammar (172 countries, 1963–2024), in particular the solidarity-response coefficient 𝛽=0.664 and the subsidiarity penalty 𝜎=0.856; (iv) it proposes a composite Magnifica Humanitas Index (MHI) for monitoring societies along the integral-human-development frontier; and (v) it translates the result into a concrete five-point policy program for the AI era. The fusion shows that Magnifica Humanitas is not only a theological document but also a precise research agenda for a humanist quantitative economics — one in which Jesuit-inspired ethical constraints become binding mathematical inequalities rather than ornamental preambles. This paper does not claim that the encyclical was influenced by the Moral Grammar; it claims only that the architectural alignment between the two texts is precise enough to be operationally useful, especially for the global network of Jesuit and Catholic universities engaged in the post-AI re-foundation of social-economic thought.

title: Multilayer Psychographic Analysis of a Personal Facebook Audience: An LLM-Assisted Pipeline with Reliability Assessment
abstract: Personal Facebook profiles host consequential publics that became effectively unmeasurable after the post-Cambridge-Analytica API lockdown. I describe an owner-authorized, API-less pipeline that recovers an engaged-audience network and a multilayer psychographic description of it from a single personal profile -- my own. The pipeline combines scrape-once/parse-many browser collection from the owner's session, two-pass LLM rubric scoring of 11,036 comments on 34 affective, moral, epistemic and political dimensions in their original languages, avatar vision scoring, topic labeling, co-engagement and reply graph construction, and the profile's own 21,199-follower dashboard as demographic ground truth. The central contribution is a validation protocol that treats the LLM as a measurement instrument: per-dimension test-retest reliability (18 of 33 dimensions pass an r >= 0.7 gate; two are retired), ordinal statistics, per-language stratification (no Russian-language penalty, with one flagged sub-gate cell), and cross-model convergence against a second frontier model family, which shows expressive-emotion constructs are model-robust while several moral-foundations dimensions are model-sensitive. A tiered de-identification design (keep / aggregate-only / drop) grounded in a k-anonymity scan yields a public release, and a 503-comment human validation with pre-registered decision rules is pending; all substantive findings are preliminary until it completes.
